figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison with CLIP-based FER and TTA methods. Results are averaged over 10 target subjects per dataset. CLIP-ViT-B/32† denotes full CLIP fine-tuning. ZS: Zero-shot ; FT: Fine-tuning ; TTA: Test-time adaptation.
Table 2: CLIP-AUTT subject-wise comparison with TTA methods on the BioVid dataset. Best results are in bold.

Table 5: We evaluate different prompt templates for representing emotion classes in the CLIP text encoder. The best-performing template, “a person with an expression of [CLS]”, is used for all methods that rely on class prompts (CPs).
Fig. 1: AU–video similarity statistics. Comparison of the mean and standard deviation of AU–video similarity logits over N epochs for (a) Transformer temporal module and (b) 1D-CNN temporal module.
Fig. 3: t-SNE visualization of video–AU similarity space between frozen clip and AU adapter.
Table 3: Subject-wise performance across 10 StressID target subjects. Metrics are grouped into WAR, UAR, and F1

Fig. 5: Qualitative comparison between CLIP-AU and CLIP-AUTT. Each pair shows the top-activated AUs, their weights, and class probabilities for sample subjects. CLIP-AUTT refines AU semantics and yields more confident, subject-aligned predictions.
Table 4: Per-subject performance across 10 BAH target subjects. Across three matrices WAR, UAR, and F1 metrics across 10 subjects.

Table 6: Full list of Class Prompts (CPs) used in the ablation study. Each emotion category replaces [CLS] with the target label (e.g., pain, stress, or ambivalence). We employ 46 templates—23 emotion-specific and 23 neutral—to capture a broad linguistic and visual context. The prompts follow CLIP-style phrasing (“a photo of a [CLS] face”, “a person with an expression of [CLS]”), ensuring alignment with large-scale vision-language pretraining.
